paragraphs.text: SUP ERC ELL
Transparency Register Identification Number: 245932993441-10

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: mobile-game company

paragraphs.text: Supercell is a Finnish mobile-game company with offices in Helsinki, London, San Francisco, Seoul, and Shanghai. Since 2010, we have launched six globally successful titles — Hay Day, Clash of Clans, Boom Beach, Clash Royale, Brawl Stars, and Squad Busters — reaching more than 5 billion downloads and 250 million monthly players in over 120 countries. In 2024, Supercell's revenue was €2.8 billion, more than one-third of which originated from Europe.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game currencies

paragraphs.text: Supercell welcomes the opportunity to respond to the Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment. We align with Video Games Europe (VGE), the association representing the European games industry. However, since especially the proposed measures on in-game currencies would have far-reaching consequences, we wish to highlight our own experience, with following key messages:

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: In Supercell's opinion there is no need for additional regulation on in-game currencies.

paragraphs.text: In Supercell's opinion there is no need for additional regulation on in-game currencies. Many of the issues raised by the Commission are already covered by existing EU legislation, some of which has only recently entered into force and lacks implementation experience. The current rules should first be tested in practice through enforcement and clarification of existing rules.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: The current proposals would require game companies to display the euro value of each in-game currency transaction when used during gameplay.

paragraphs.text: The free-to-play model is an established business model in the industry, and in-game currencies—earned through gameplay and in some cases purchased with real money—are a fundamental part of it. The current proposals would require game companies to display the euro value of each in-game currency transaction when used during gameplay. This reflects a misunderstanding of how games function and would jeopardize the viability of the entire model.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

paragraphs.text: Consumer-protection legislation should remain horizontal, general and proportionate in nature. Excessively detailed or overlapping rules risk creating a fragmented and unmanageable framework for consumers, companies, and authorities alike. New regulation should only be considered when a genuine and substantiated legal gap has been identified.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

paragraphs.text: Player protection is at the core of Supercell's business. Our mission is to create games that are played by as many people as possible, for years to come, and that are remembered forever. To achieve this, a positive player experience—including consumer protection—is central to everything we do.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

paragraphs.text: SUPERCELL

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game currencies

paragraphs.text: No need for additional regulation on in-game currencies

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: In-Game Currencies as the Foundation of the Free-to-Play Model

paragraphs.text: 1.1. In-Game Currencies as the Foundation of the Free-to-Play Model

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: In-game currencies—such as coins and gems which are typically used within the gameplay as game-pieces—form the foundation of this model.

paragraphs.text: Free-to-play games allow millions of people to try and enjoy games at no cost. In-game currencies—such as coins and gems which are typically used within the gameplay as game-pieces—form the foundation of this model. They are not virtual or financial currencies, but a part of the game's digital content and gameplay. They cannot be converted into real money or used outside the game.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: purchase in-game currency with real money

paragraphs.text: In Supercell's games, players may choose to purchase in-game currency with real money, but all games remain fully playable without payment. Over 80 percent of Supercell players have never spent any money in our games.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: Purchases always take place in a clearly separate in-game store
every transaction requires explicit confirmation
prices are shown in euros (or other local currency)

paragraphs.text: Purchases always take place in a clearly separate in-game store, where prices are shown in euros (or other local currency) and every transaction requires explicit confirmation. This ensures full transparency and informed decision-making by the player.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

paragraphs.text: 1.2. Proposed Rules Would Undermine the Player Experience

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: proposal that each use of in-game currency should display its value in euros

paragraphs.text: The European Commission's and the CPC Network's proposal that each use of in-game currency should display its value in euros demonstrates a misunderstanding of how games function and how players experience them.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: confirm price information dozens of times during a single play session

paragraphs.text: In practice, this would mean that a player would need to confirm price information dozens of times during a single play session — comparable to being asked to accept cookie banners on every webpage.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: undermine gameplay

paragraphs.text: Such a rule would not strengthen consumer protection, but would severely undermine gameplay and make the free-to-play model in Europe practically impossible.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

paragraphs.text: 1.3. Players Spend Responsibly and Understand Value

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

paragraphs.text: Supercell's player data clearly shows that our players spend responsibly:

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: in-app purchase

paragraphs.text: 84% of players who played our games in the past 12 months have never made a single in-app purchase.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

paragraphs.text: The average transaction value in September 2025 was €5.47.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: For clarity, none of these refund requests were based on confusion about the nature or value of in-game currencies.

paragraphs.text: In September 2025, only 0.6% of transactions were refunded, representing 0.85% of in-app revenue. For clarity, none of these refund requests were based on confusion about the nature or value of in-game currencies.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

paragraphs.text: SUPERCELL

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: player spending is limited, intentional, and under the player's control.
in-game currencies do not lead to confusion or uncontrolled spending.

paragraphs.text: These figures demonstrate that player spending is limited, intentional, and under the player's control. They also confirm that in-game currencies do not lead to confusion or uncontrolled spending.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game currencies
Prices are always displayed clearly in both euros and game tokens
every transaction requires explicit confirmation
review their purchase history at any time via app-store settings or within the game
a receipt through app-store billing system on all real-money purchases made

paragraphs.text: From a behavioural perspective, there is no credible evidence that in-game currencies cause players to lose awareness of real-world value. Prices are always displayed clearly in both euros and game tokens (for example, “50 diamonds = €2.49”), and every transaction requires explicit confirmation. Players can also review their purchase history at any time via app-store settings or within the game. Players also receive a receipt through app-store billing system on all real-money purchases made.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: The visible in-game balance acts as a clear, finite budget — comparable to a prepaid card — allowing players to monitor and plan their spending.
Supercell's games include multiple layers of confirmation, cooling-off mechanics, and parental controls, all of which promote informed decision-making rather than impulsive behaviour.

paragraphs.text: The visible in-game balance acts as a clear, finite budget — comparable to a prepaid card — allowing players to monitor and plan their spending. Supercell's games include multiple layers of confirmation, cooling-off mechanics, and parental controls, all of which promote informed decision-making rather than impulsive behaviour.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game currencies as part of the gameplay experience

paragraphs.text: Our experience and market data indicate that players understand the role of in-game currencies as part of the gameplay experience and do not confuse them with real-world money. Misunderstandings about value are exceedingly rare among hundreds of millions of active players.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game currencies are digital content, not financial instruments.

paragraphs.text: The legal nature of in-game currencies is well established in EU law. EU case law and regulatory guidance have consistently recognized that in-game currencies are digital content, not financial instruments.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
1.4. Reinterpreting in-game currencies would weaken consumer protection

evidence_quotes.quote: the purchases of in-game currencies

paragraphs.text: Reinterpreting them otherwise would weaken, not strengthen, consumer protection. Currently the purchases of in-game currencies already fall under the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD) and the Digital Content Directive (DCD), which guarantee players key rights such as conformity and withdrawal. Reclassification would deprive players of these protections and create significant legal uncertainty. Existing EU legislation already covers these matters comprehensively — additional regulation is unnecessary.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
1.4. Reinterpreting in-game currencies would weaken consumer protection

paragraphs.text: SUPERCELL OY I JÄTKÄSAARENLAITURI 1, FI-00180 HELSINKI, FINLAND | SUPERCELL.COM | 2336509-6

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
1.4. Reinterpreting in-game currencies would weaken consumer protection

paragraphs.text: SUP ERCELL

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
1.4. Reinterpreting in-game currencies would weaken consumer protection

paragraphs.text: Supercell considers that consumer-protection should remain horizontal and generally applicable, not sector-specific. This approach ensures regulatory consistency and a level playing field across the EU.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
2. Consumer-protection legislation should remain horizontal and general

paragraphs.text: The threshold for new legislative initiatives must be high. The EU already has an extensive legal framework — including the CRD, DCD, DSA, DMA, UCPD, AIA, and GDPR — whose coherent implementation provides a solid basis for digital consumer protection. New regulation should only be introduced when a real legislative gap has been identified, not to compensate for enforcement or coordination challenges.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
2. Consumer-protection legislation should remain horizontal and general

paragraphs.text: Efforts to strengthen consumer protection should avoid fragmenting the regulatory landscape or creating unnecessary burdens for European businesses. The EU should also ensure that consumer-, data-, and digital-protection laws remain compatible and technologically neutral.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
2. Consumer-protection legislation should remain horizontal and general

paragraphs.text: Rather than adopting a new Digital Fairness Act, the Commission should focus on effective enforcement and practical guidance under the existing framework.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
2. Consumer-protection legislation should remain horizontal and general

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game currencies

paragraphs.text: Given this evidence, any new regulation concerning in-game currencies would not meet the EU's principle of proportionality. The alleged problem has not been supported by empirical data or measurable consumer detriment. Introducing new obligations would therefore represent a disproportionate response to a hypothetical risk.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
3. Regulation Must Be Proportionate to Demonstrable Harm

evidence_quotes.quote: Imposing financial-style regulation on game tokens would dismantle transparent, functioning systems that already empower players to manage their spending responsibly.

paragraphs.text: Such measures would not enhance consumer protection—they would undermine it. Imposing financial-style regulation on game tokens would dismantle transparent, functioning systems that already empower players to manage their spending responsibly. It would also divert enforcement resources away from genuine consumer risks and discourage innovation within Europe's thriving creative-tech sector.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
3. Regulation Must Be Proportionate to Demonstrable Harm

paragraphs.text: A proportionate approach should instead focus on testing the existing legislation through enforcement and supporting effective self- and co-regulatory measures. Regulating against speculative harms, in the absence of evidence, would impose unnecessary burdens on both players and developers—ultimately harming the very consumers such measures seek to protect.

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
3. Regulation Must Be Proportionate to Demonstrable Harm

paragraphs.text: SUPERCELL OY I JÄTKÄSAARENLAITURI 1, FI-00180 HELSINKI, FINLAND I SUPERCELL.COM I 2336509-6

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
3. Regulation Must Be Proportionate to Demonstrable Harm

paragraphs.text: SUP ERCELL

section_path: Public Consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact assessment
3. Regulation Must Be Proportionate to Demonstrable Harm

evidence_quotes.quote: artificial urgency
rewards decrease after about 45 minutes of continuous play,

paragraphs.text: At Supercell, players' wellbeing and a safe, enjoyable experience are central to everything we do. Our games are designed to be fun and engaging without pressure or artificial urgency. For example, in *Brawl Stars*, rewards decrease after about 45 minutes of continuous play, encouraging balanced gameplay. Similarly, no in-game offers are available for less than 48 hours, preventing "limited-time" pressure tactics.

section_path: 4. Consideration of players, parents, and responsible design

evidence_quotes.quote: Children and young players benefit from built-in protections and age-appropriate settings, while parents are provided with clear tools and guidance at supercell.com/parents to help them manage gameplay and in-app purchases.
"Have a conversation with your child about gaming"

paragraphs.text: We also take age and parental oversight seriously. Children and young players benefit from built-in protections and age-appropriate settings, while parents are provided with clear tools and guidance at supercell.com/parents to help them manage gameplay and in-app purchases. Our Safety Checklist encourages parents to start with open communication—"Have a conversation with your child about gaming"—so that families can set expectations and boundaries together. It also includes guidance on how to check or restrict in-app purchase settings on a child's device, helping parents ensure that spending is always transparent and intentional. We also explain how to use parental controls provided by Apple, Google, and other platforms to turn off or limit in-app purchases entirely, if desired. These are standard practices used in the industry.

section_path: 4. Consideration of players, parents, and responsible design

evidence_quotes.quote: the wider games industry

paragraphs.text: However, we are concerned that companies' own responsible practices and self-regulatory efforts—developed over years by Supercell and the wider games industry—are not sufficiently recognized in the ongoing regulatory discussions. Reinforcing these proven practices would often achieve the same objectives as legislation.

section_path: 4. Consideration of players, parents, and responsible design

evidence_quotes.quote: new rules threaten the vitality of the European games sector.

paragraphs.text: Supercell supports the Commission's goal of strong consumer protection but stresses that the goal is right, the means are wrong if new rules threaten the vitality of the European games sector.

section_path: 5. Conclusion

paragraphs.text: We wish:

section_path: 5. Conclusion

evidence_quotes.quote: No new regulation on in-game currencies

paragraphs.text: No new regulation on in-game currencies: They are the foundation of the free-to-play model and are already comprehensively regulated.

section_path: 5. Conclusion

evidence_quotes.quote: enforcing existing rules
issuing clear guidance

paragraphs.text: Horizontal and proportionate approach: Instead of introducing new legislation, the Commission should focus on enforcing existing rules and issuing clear guidance.

section_path: 5. Conclusion

paragraphs.text: SUP ERCELL

section_path: 5. Conclusion

paragraphs.text: For Europe's competitiveness and digital sovereignty, it is essential that regulation is based on evidence, proportionality, and the reinforcement of effective practices—not on creating new, overlapping obligations.

section_path: 5. Conclusion

paragraphs.text: Supercell remains committed to constructive dialogue with policymakers and authorities to achieve our shared goal: a safe, fair, and globally competitive digital Europe.

section_path: 5. Conclusion

paragraphs.text: In Helsinki 23 October, 2025
